The witnesses agree the substance: Samson, willing to die, pulls down the house and gains his greatest victory in death (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of victory through death: the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life, the deliverer conquering by dying, a dim type of Christ who through death destroyed him that had the power of death (Hebrews 2:14). The verse displays the victory-through-death crux. Samson's death and his greatest victory display the crux: Let me die with the Philistines... So the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life (John 12:24; Hebrews 2:14) — the victory wrought through the death of God's servant, the deliverer who conquers the foe by dying with them. The verse displays Samson's brethren coming down to take his body and bury him (vv.31).
31:
"Then his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the buryingplace of Manoah his father. And he judged Israel twenty years." The very brief verse notes Samson's burial by his kindred, having judged Israel twenty years. The grammatical voices note the burial. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him... and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol... And he judged Israel twenty years" — Samson was buried in his father's grave, his twenty years' judgeship ended; the then his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the buryingplace of Manoah his father (the burial); the And he judged Israel twenty years (the judgeship); the figure (the deliverer laid to rest among his fathers, the close of his troubled course; the truth that Samson, who judged twenty years, was buried in his father's grave, Hebrews 11:32; the figure of the deliverer's course ended and his rest entered, the judge numbered among the faithful; the burial of Samson; the twenty years, vv.30). The verse closes the history of Samson. The verse displays the close of the deliverer's life, his body borne home and laid among his fathers, his twenty-year judgeship complete. The verse displays the truth that Samson, for all his falls, is numbered among the heroes of faith, the deliverer God raised up and owned to the end.

On "Then his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the buryingplace of Manoah his father," the witnesses note the burial. Gill notes Samson buried in his father's grave (the burial, Hebrews 11:32) — the burial (between Zorah and Eshtaol).

On "And he judged Israel twenty years," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the judgeship: "he judged Israel twenty years" — Samson's twenty-year rule (the term); the figure (the deliverer's course ended and his rest entered); the providence (the burial, the history of Micah's idolatry to follow, ch.17). The verse displays Samson's burial and twenty years' judgeship.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the burial — "his brethren... buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol... And he judged Israel twenty years," the burial (Keil, Gill); "Then his brethren and all the house of his father came down, and took him, and brought him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the buryingplace of Manoah his father," the burial (Hebrews 11:32, Gill); "And he judged Israel twenty years," the judgeship (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burial. The verse marks Samson: buried. The verse displays the kindred: bearing him home. The verse displays the grave: his father's. The verse displays the years: twenty. The burial displays the deliverer's course ended and his rest entered, the judge numbered among the faithful. The verse displays the deliverer: at rest. The verse displays the course: complete. The verse displays the faith: owned. Samson's burial and twenty years' judgeship display the deliverer's course ended and his rest entered: his brethren... buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol...
